skill.name: cursor-mem
skill.description: Connect Cursor to the claude-mem MCP server to search past session memory and record new project observations.
skill.summary: Search and write shared claude-mem records from Cursor, Claude Code, and Codex workflows.
skill.author: Chachamaru127

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, filesystem, network, weak crypto, and obfuscation patterns. Review found no prompt injection, malicious payload, credential exfiltration, or remote code execution intent; most hits are Markdown examples, local setup commands, localhost health checks, documentation links, Japanese text entropy, and relative documentation links. The skill still warrants a medium warning because it intentionally connects to MCP tools, allows Bash, and reads or writes a shared local claude-mem SQLite database that may contain sensitive project memory.

medium_findings.title: Shared Local Memory Database Access
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally uses the same claude-mem SQLite database for Cursor and Claude Code. This is functional for the skill, but records may contain private project decisions, reviews, bug history, and handoff notes, so users need clear data handling expectations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly names the local SQLite database path and warns users to be careful with sensitive information. The behavior is intentional, but the privacy risk is real.

medium_findings.title: Bash and Local MCP Tooling Require User Trust
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash and wildcard claude-mem MCP tools, and setup guidance includes chmod, curl, and node commands for local integration. These are legitimate setup and troubleshooting commands, but the permission set can run local commands if invoked by an assistant.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Bash access is explicit in the skill metadata and local commands are shown in troubleshooting. I did not find malicious command construction or exfiltration, so this is a publication warning rather than a block.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Mostly Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command detections are backticks around MCP tool names, JSON examples, or fenced walkthrough text. They describe how to use claude-mem and do not execute by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are documentation blocks or inline tool names, not executable source code. A few setup commands remain relevant and are covered in the medium findings.

low_findings.title: Network Detections Are Localhost Checks or Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded network values are a localhost health check and public documentation links for claude-mem and Cursor MCP setup. I did not find evidence that the skill sends memory contents to a remote service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only IP address is 127.0.0.1 and the URLs point to documentation. No evidence found of outbound exfiltration endpoints.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Weak Crypto Hits Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The path traversal detections are relative links to documentation, and the weak crypto detections appear to come from Markdown file extensions or ordinary technical terms. I did not find evidence of cryptographic implementation or path traversal logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation metadata or relative links, not code that opens user-controlled paths or implements cryptography. No evidence found of traversal execution.

low_findings.title: Entropy and Dangerous Combination Heuristics Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The scanner reported high entropy and a dangerous command, network, and credential combination. Review found Japanese prose, Markdown examples, localhost tooling, and local database references, but no encoded payload or credential access pattern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding content is readable documentation and examples. No evidence found of hidden instructions, encoded code, credential scraping, or prompt injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowed Bash Permission
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill metadata grants Bash access. This is not malicious by itself, but marketplace users should understand that assistants may run local setup or diagnostic commands when using the skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission is explicit in the front matter. Risk depends on assistant behavior and user approval model, not on a malicious instruction found in the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Hidden Database Path
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents use of a hidden home-directory SQLite database for shared memory. This can store sensitive project context and should be treated as local private data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The database path is explicitly documented. It supports the skill purpose but creates a clear privacy consideration.

content.user_title: Share Project Memory Across AI Tools
content.value_statement: Teams lose context when decisions and review notes stay inside one assistant session. This skill connects Cursor to claude-mem so Claude, Codex, and Claude Code can search and record shared project memory.
actual_capabilities: Search claude-mem records from Cursor using MCP tools.
Retrieve recent context, timelines, and individual observations.
Record design decisions, review findings, patterns, bugs, and handoff notes.
Use shared tags for Cursor and Claude Code memory entries.
Guide setup for a local claude-mem MCP server in Cursor.
limitations: Requires an existing claude-mem MCP server and Cursor MCP configuration.
Does not create or manage the claude-mem database by itself.
Memory quality depends on clear tags and concise observations.
Sensitive information should not be stored without user policy approval.

use_cases.title: Review Decision History
use_cases.description: Search past architecture and product decisions before approving a change or writing new requirements.

use_cases.title: Reuse Implementation Patterns
use_cases.description: Find previous validation, authentication, migration, or component patterns before implementing similar work.

use_cases.title: Prepare Cross-Tool Handoffs
use_cases.description: Record handoff notes in Cursor so Claude Code or Codex can continue with the same project context.

prompt_templates.title: Search a Past Decision
prompt_templates.prompt: Search claude-mem for why we chose [technology or architecture choice]. Return the most relevant decision notes and dates.

prompt_templates.title: Record a Review Finding
prompt_templates.prompt: Record this review finding in claude-mem with source:cursor and type:review tags: [finding]. Include the affected area and next action.

prompt_templates.title: Find Reusable Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Search project memory for prior patterns related to [feature or bug]. Summarize what should be reused and any files mentioned.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Handoff Trail
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a claude-mem handoff entry for [workstream]. Include current status, blockers, decisions, useful tags, and what Claude Code or Codex should do next.

output_examples.input: Search memory for the authentication decision.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns the relevant decision entry, explains the reason Supabase Auth was selected, and lists matching tags.

output_examples.input: Record this RLS review pattern for future work.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a review observation with source and pattern tags, then confirms the stored topic and retrieval terms.

output_examples.input: Prepare a backend error handling handoff.
output_examples.output: The assistant records a handoff note with status, problem context, next steps, and search tags for the implementation assistant.

best_practices: Use consistent tags such as source:cursor, type:decision, type:pattern, and type:handoff.
Record why a decision was made, not only what changed.
Review memory content before storing sensitive project or customer information.
anti_patterns: Do not store secrets, tokens, passwords, or private customer data in memory records.
Do not create vague entries without tags or retrieval keywords.
Do not use this skill for ordinary coding tasks that do not need shared memory.

faq.question: What does this skill connect?
faq.answer: It connects Cursor workflows to a claude-mem MCP server for shared memory search and writing.

faq.question: Does it work without claude-mem installed?
faq.answer: No. You need a working claude-mem MCP server and Cursor MCP configuration.

faq.question: Can Claude Code and Cursor share the same records?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is designed around a shared local claude-mem SQLite database.

faq.question: Is network access required?
faq.answer: The documented health check uses localhost. Documentation links point to external reference pages.

faq.question: What information should not be stored?
faq.answer: Do not store secrets, credentials, regulated data, or customer information without approval.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill intentionally uses Bash-capable setup steps and a shared local memory database that may contain sensitive context.
